coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites for photocatalytic degradation of veterinary pharmaceutical sarafloxacin
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Jie
He, Qiang
Zhang, Xiaolong
Guo, Xiao
Song, Qiang
Liu, Yan
Yao, Binghua
Zhang, Qian
Dionysiou, Dionysios D.
coredata.dc:description: The unabated release of fluoroquinolone compounds into surface water led to environmental problems. In this contribution, the heterogeneous photocatalytic technology was employed utilizing carbon quantum dot CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites for degrading the veterinary pharmaceutical sarafloxacin as a model pollutant. A simple hydrothermal method was used to synthesize CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites with different CQDs amount (1–4 wt%). X-ray diffraction and Fourier transform infrared spectra revealed the formation of CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites. UV–vis spectra of CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites displayed a marked absorption enhancement in the range of 400–700 nm. Transmission electron microscopy images demonstrated the as-prepared CQDs having small particle size were successfully coupled with Bi5Nb3O15. X-ray photoelectron spectra indicated the presence of C, O, Bi and Nb in CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller measurements confirmed that the surface area of 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites was higher than that of Bi5Nb3O15. The photocatalytic decomposition of sarafloxacin was investigated using these novel nanocomposites under irradiation of 250 W Xe lamp. The prepared 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites exhibited better degradation efficiency than pure Bi5Nb3O15 nanoparticles. In addition, the degradation products were identified by UHPLC/MS/MS and possible degradation pathways were proposed. Four new intermediates were found, featuring a series of steps including cleavage of piperazine ring and oxidation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15
Veterinary pharmaceutical
Sarafloxacin
Degradation
Water treatment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Influence of the initial sarafloxacin concentration and photocatalysts dosage

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Shaanxi Provincial Science and Technology Department
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed degradation pathway of sarafloxacin under visible light illumination using 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites.
Schematic illustration for the photocatalytic degradation process over 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites.
XRD patterns of CQDs, Bi5Nb3O15 and CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites with different CQDs content.
FTIR spectra of CQDs, Bi5Nb3O15 and CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites with different contents of CQDs (b) the plot of (αhν)2 versus hν.
TEM images of (a) Bi5Nb3O15, (b) CQDs, (c–e) 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites at different magnifications and (f) HRTEM images of 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites.
XPS spectra of Bi5Nb3O15 and 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites: (a) full survey, (b) Bi 4f, (c) Nb 3d, (d) O 1s and (e) C 1s.
The transient photocurrent response of Bi5Nb3O15 and 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites.
(a) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms, (b) pore size distribution curves of Bi5Nb3O15 and 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites.
Comparison of the photocatalytic activities for the degradation of sarafloxacin over the prepared photocatalysts.
(a) Effect of initial concentration of sarafloxacin on its k app (min−1) and removal rate (mM min−1) and (b) Effect of catalyst loading on sarafloxacin degradation.
UHPLC chromatograms at different time intervals for the degradation of sarafloxacin.
(a) Reusability of the 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites for the degradation of sarafloxacin and (b) The original and the used XRD spectra of the 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites.
Photocatalytic degradation of sarafloxacin in pure water and real water under visible light irradiation.
Parameters obtained from N2 adsorption-desorption measurements.
Kinetic parameters based on different initial concentrations of sarafloxacin (3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 = 2 g L−1, 250 W xenon lamp).
Kinetic parameters for sarafloxacin degradation based on different amount of 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites (C0 = 0.04 mM, 250 W xenon lamp).

head.ce:title: Fabrication of CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites for photocatalytic degradation of veterinary pharmaceutical sarafloxacin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Xi’an University of Technology, 5 Jinhua South Road, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710048, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technical Center, Shaanxi Entry-Exit Inspection and Quarantine Bureau, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710068, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Entry-Exit Inspection and Quarantine Bureau

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental Engineering and Science Program, Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering (ChEE), University of Cincinnati, Cincinnati, OH 45221, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering (ChEE)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The unabated release of fluoroquinolone compounds into surface water led to environmental problems. In this contribution, the heterogeneous photocatalytic technology was employed utilizing carbon quantum dot CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites for degrading the veterinary pharmaceutical sarafloxacin as a model pollutant. A simple hydrothermal method was used to synthesize CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites with different CQDs amount (1–4 wt%). X-ray diffraction and Fourier transform infrared spectra revealed the formation of CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites. UV–vis spectra of CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites displayed a marked absorption enhancement in the range of 400–700 nm. Transmission electron microscopy images demonstrated the as-prepared CQDs having small particle size were successfully coupled with Bi5Nb3O15. X-ray photoelectron spectra indicated the presence of C, O, Bi and Nb in CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller measurements confirmed that the surface area of 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites was higher than that of Bi5Nb3O15. The photocatalytic decomposition of sarafloxacin was investigated using these novel nanocomposites under irradiation of 250 W Xe lamp. The prepared 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites exhibited better degradation efficiency than pure Bi5Nb3O15 nanoparticles. In addition, the degradation products were identified by UHPLC/MS/MS and possible degradation pathways were proposed. Four new intermediates were found, featuring a series of steps including cleavage of piperazine ring and oxidation.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few years, human and veterinary medicine residues have been found in surface water [1]. Fluoroquinolone antibiotics are the most frequently detected antibiotics in water [2]. Sarafloxacin [1-(p-fluorophenyl)-6-fluoro-1,4-dihydro-4-oxo-7-(piperazinyl)-quinoline-3-carboxylic acid], belongs to fluoroquinolone antibiotics. It is extensively applied in veterinary medicine or aquaculture in order to prevent and treat some diseases [3]. Research has shown that trace amounts of sarafloxacin in aqueous environment is mainly derived from animal excretion [4,5]. Its continuous input poses risk to antibiotic resistance for animals, humans and the ecological environment [2]. Moreover, sarafloxacin is prone to bind on organic matter and is somewhat resistant to degradation [6,7]. Therefore, treatment of this compound is a topic of significant interest among researchers. Different methods including photolysis and biodegradation have been applied for the removal of sarafloxacin. Unfortunately, these methods are ineffective for the treatment of sarafloxacin. Nowadays, the heterogeneous photochemical treatment methods are considered as effective methods for the decomposition of antibiotic contaminants [8]. Such process can degrade sarafloxacin to smaller molecules through special photochemical reaction. However, research studies focused on semiconductor-assisted photocatalytic degradation of sarafloxacin are seldom described in the literature. Hence, the design of efficient photocatalysts and deploying them in the photocatalytic degradation has been an area of interest.

Niobate-based semiconductors are attractive because of their unique ferroelectric, optoelectronic, electrochemical and photocatalytic properties [9,10]. Many of niobate photocatalysts, such as AgNbO3 [11], Bi3NbO7 [9], Pb2Nb2O7 [12] and KNbO3 [13] have been extensively studied. Among the different niobate semiconductors, bismuth niobate (Bi5Nb3O15) exhibits excellent photoresponse characteristics. K. Gurunathan and P. Maruthamuthu found that Bi5Nb3O15 could be used as a photocatalyst for the first time [14]. Since then, various methods have been applied to synthesize Bi5Nb3O15 and evaluate its performance for environmental applications. The classical method used to synthesize Bi5Nb3O15 is based on a solid-state reaction [15]. This method is convenient for the preparation of Bi5Nb3O15. Nevertheless, the starting materials cannot always mix homogenously, resulting in a product with large crystal size, small surface area, and relatively low activity. The development of sol-gel technique has provided valuable means for preparing Bi5Nb3O15 with small crystal size and higher photocatalytic activity [16]. Besides, the technique of hydrothermal reaction has been applied successfully for the synthesis of Bi5Nb3O15 [17]. This is an effective method for obtaining high performance photocatalysts because it can be performed using a simple reaction apparatus, it requires less complex experimental procedures, and it can be carried out under mild reaction conditions [18]. It has been reported that the band gap of Bi5Nb3O15 catalyst is 2.92 eV [17]. It can only absorb slight amount of visible light, making it difficult for practical solar application. To broaden its photoactivity towards visible light, Bi5Nb3O15 has been doped [19] or combined with other materials [20,21]. Although the band gap of Bi5Nb3O15 could be narrowed, the photo-generated charge carriers still recombined fast. In this perspective, developing novel Bi5Nb3O15-based photocatalysts with superior electron transfer capacity and enhanced photocatalytic efficiency still remains a challenge.

In recent decades, special carbon nanoparticle CQDs have gained much attention. CQDs have low toxicity, wide light absorption spectrum range, ultra-large absorption coefficient, particular photon-upconversion and electron separation ability [22]. Owing to their unique characteristics, CQDs could be used as promising candidates for photocatalytic applications [23]. The design of CQDs with different metal-oxide semiconductors, for example, CQDs/TiO2 [24], CQDs/Bi2MoO6 [25], CQDs/Bi20TiO32 [26], CQDs/Ag3PO4 [27], CQDs/BiOBr [28] and others, provide opportunities to develop excellent photocatalysts for application in the degradation of antibiotic residues in source waters. Considering the special properties of CQDs, it was hypothesized in this work that coupling of Bi5Nb3O15 with CQDs could potentially improve the photocatalytic activity of Bi5Nb3O15. As per the literature survey, preparation of CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites has not yet been reported.

Based on this idea, CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites were fabricated by a simple step hydrothermal method. A series of CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites were fabricated using different weight content of CQDs and Bi5Nb3O15. The crystal structures, morphologies, physical and photo-electrochemical activities of the prepared samples were studied in detail. The prepared CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites can be applied as an efficient visible light photocatalyst to degrade the veterinary antibiotic sarafloxacin in surface water. The mechanism of semiconductor-assisted photocatalytic degradation sarafloxacin was also investigated.

All the chemicals and reagents used in this process were of analytical grade and were used as received. Bismuth nitrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), niobium chloride (NbCl5), citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O), urea (H2NCONH2), ethanol (C2H5OH), potassium hydroxide (KOH), and ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O) were obtained from China SCR company. Sarafloxacin hydrochloride trihydrate standard (C20H17F2N3O3·HCl·3H2O, 97.2%) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Deionized water was purified through a MUL-9000 Series instrument (18.2 MΩ cm, Kunshan Multiply Industrial Company, China).

CQDs were synthesized according to a modified literature method [29]. In a typical process, 3.0 g of citric acid and 2.1 g of urea were dissolved in 30 mL water using a sonic bath for 30 min. Then, the above homogeneous mixture was pour in a 50 mL Teflon-lined reactor and was placed in an oven at 180 °C for 5.0 h. After cooling to room temperature, a 0.45 μm membrane was used to purify the dark brown solution, followed by centrifugation at 11,000 rpm for 30 min. Further, the CQDs solution was dialyzed through MW500 membrane for at least 2 days. Finally, the freeze-drying technique was applied to collect the CQDs powders.

The Bi5Nb3O15 sample was prepared by a hydrothermal method [21]. Typically, 2 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 1.2 mmol of NbCl5 were dissolved in 20 mL anhydrous ethanol separately. Then, the two solutions were mixed. During mixing, suitable amount of 1 M KOH solution was added until the pH reached 10. After filtering and washing with deionized water, excess amount of salts was removed to obtain a precipitate. The above precipitate was dispersed in 30 mL water and NH3·H2O was added slowly until pH to 9. The prepared slurry was stirred by a magnetic mixer for 30 min. Further, the slurry was put into a 50 mL Teflon vessel reactor and reacted at 220 °C for 24.0 h. The products were filtered, washed and dried in air at 50 °C for a day.

The CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites were combined via a hydrothermal method. Briefly, 200 mg of Bi5Nb3O15 powder was dispersed in the previously prepared 30 mL CQDs solution and nitric acid was added to adjust the solution pH to 3. After magnetic stirring for 30 min, the suspension was pour into a 50 mL Teflon-lined reactor and heated at 140 °C for 24.0 h. The powder was separated by filtration through distilled water and dried at 50 °C for 24.0 h. The products were labeled as 1 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15, 2 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15, 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 and 4 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15, representing the mass content of CQDs with respect to Bi5Nb3O15. To compare the pure Bi5Nb3O15 and CQDs modified Bi5Nb3O15, the pure Bi5Nb3O15 was treated using the same procedure as stated above. The product was labeled as Bi5Nb3O15-1.

A Shimadzu XRD-7000 s diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ =1.5406 nm) and the 2θ range from 10° to 60° under scanning rate of 0.02°/sec was used to determine the XRD spectra. The TEM investigations were performed by a Japan JEM 3010 electron microscope. FT-IR spectra were conducted using a SHIMADZU FT-IR 8000 photometer in the frequency range 400-4000 cm−1. The UV–vis absorption spectra were determined using Beijing Purkinje TU-1901 spectrophotometer in the region between 200–800 nm wavelengths. The JW-BK-122W Beijing JWGB instrument was applied to test the surface areas and porosity of the prepared catalysts. The surface area was calculated by the BET method. The pore size distribution was deduced from the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda desorption data. The XPS analysis was displayed on a Kratos AXIS-ULTRADLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The XPS Peak 4 Software was used to analyze the obtained data. C 1s at 284.8 eV as the charge compensation was used to calibrate the binding energy. A Shanghai CHEN HUA CHI660b electrochemical workstation with a conventional three electrode system was applied to measure the photoelectrochemical signal. The working, counter and reference electrode were an ITO substrate coated with prepared catalysts, a Pt wire and a saturated Ag/AgCl, respectively. A 250 W xenon lamp and 0.1 mol L-1 Na2SO4 solution was also used in the experiment.

Sarafloxacin in solution was used as a model fluoroquinolone antibiotic pollutant to examine the photocatalytic activities of the prepared materials. Before measurement, 0.1 g catalyst was suspended in 50 mL of 15 mg L−1 sarafloxacin (initial pH 5.56) and the suspension was kept in dark for about 30 min. Afterwards the suspension was illuminated with a 250 W Xe lamp. During the experiment, 1 mL of the solution was withdrawn from the reaction solution and purified by 0.22 μm filters prior to ultra-high performance liquid chromatography (UHPLC) analysis.

A Dionex Ultimate 3000 UHPLC with a Thermo Hypersil Gold aQ column (100 mm × 2.1 mm, 1.9 μm) with column temperature at 35 °C was used to measure the concentration of sarafloxacin in degradation solution. A gradient elution method with 0.1% formic acid in water as mobile solution A and acetonitrile as mobile solution B was applied for the chromatographic analysis. The setup of the gradient chromatography method was as follows: 0–1 min, 10% solution B; 1–4 min, 10–80% solution B (using gradient curve 3); 4–6 min, hold 80% solution B (using gradient curve 5); 6–8 min, hold 10% solution B. The sample of 10 μL was injected and mixed with the mobile phase at 0.3 mL min−1. A Thermo Fisher company Q Exactive™ hybrid Quadrupole-Orbitrap high-resolution mass, equipped with a heated electrospray ionization source (HESI-II) operated in the positive ion mode was performed to detect the products. The Orbitrap was operated in Full MS-ddMS2 mode at resolution 70000, IT 200 ms, with a scan range from 300 to 500 m/z. Source conditions were: spray voltage 3.8 kV, capillary temperature 320 °C, sheath gas pressure 40 psi, auxiliary gas pressure 10 bars, S-Lens voltage 55 V. Thermo calibration solutions were used to calibrated the instrument before analysis.

The crystal structure, phase composition and purity of the catalysts were determined by XRD analysis. As shown in Fig. 1 , a broad diffraction peak near 26° was attributed to the intrinsic amorphous property of CQDs. The diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 29.14°, 32.78°, 33.74°, 47.08°, 48.46°, 56.10° corresponding to the crystal plane of (114), (020), (008), (220), (028), (134), respectively, indicated the formation of orthorhombic Bi5Nb3O15 (JCPDS 55-0528). For the CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites, the XRD spectra showed identical characteristic diffraction peaks as Bi5Nb3O15 and no typical diffraction peak of carbon was detected. In addition, the diffraction intensity of the CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites slightly decreased. This was probably due to the amorphous attribute of CQDs, high dispersion and little amounts of CQDs in the final material [30].

The presence of CQDs in the CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites and the interaction between CQDs and Bi5Nb3O15 were identified by FTIR spectroscopic studies as shown in Fig. 2 . Based on the information of the IR spectrum of pure CQDs, the sharp peaks at 1716 cm−1, 1577 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 were attributed to stretching vibrations of CO, CC and C–O group, respectively [31,32]. Bi5Nb3O15 spectrum displayed two main bands at 3458 cm−1 and 624 cm−1 corresponding to vibrations of OH bonds from surface absorbed water and NbO6 octahedron, respectively [33,34]. FT-IR revealed that the main IR peaks in the spectra of CQDs and Bi5Nb3O15 were present in the spectrum of CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites. Moreover, the characteristic peak at 1577 cm−1 shifted to 1610 cm-1 in CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites IR spectra, indicating the interaction between CQDs and Bi5Nb3O15 in the nanocomposites.

UV–vis spectra were used to characterize the optical properties of Bi5Nb3O15 and CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites. As displayed in Fig. 3 , a clear absorption edge at 437 nm for pure Bi5Nb3O15 could be observed, which is ascribed to the band-gap transition from hybrid orbitals of O 2p and Bi 6s to Nb 4d orbitals [20]. In contrast to pure Bi5Nb3O15, the CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites showed a marked absorption enhancement in the visible light region. The observed results showed that 3 wt% CQDs/ Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites exhibited the strongest radiation absorption in the range from 400 to 700 nm.

The Eg value of the obtained samples was determined by employing Tauc relationship as given below [35]:
(1) αhν=A(hν-Eg ) n
where α is absorption coefficient, ν is light frequency, A is an empirical constant, E g is band gap energy, h is Planck’s constant and n = 1/2 for direct transition and n = 2 for indirect transition. In the Tauc equation as shown in Eq. (1), n = 1/2 was used to calculate the direct band gap energies for the prepared samples [36]. The value of E g was deduced from extrapolating the linear portion of (αhν)2 to 0, which corresponded to the effective band gap value. As shown in Fig. 3b, the direct E g values of Bi5Nb3O15 and 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites were calculated to be 2.94 eV and 2.79 eV, respectively. It was confirmed that coupling CQDs with Bi5Nb3O15 can result in increase of its absorption ability at a considerable extent.

The morphology and microstructure of CQDs, Bi5Nb3O15 and 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites were investigated using TEM analysis. As displayed in Fig. 4 a, the synthesized CQDs presented sphere-like shape with a mean size of 3 nm. No apparent aggregation can be observed. Fig. 4b displayed TEM image of Bi5Nb3O15 which exhibited irregular shape nanosheets with 30–80 nm particle size. The TEM images of 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites at various magnifications (Fig. 4c-e) showed the particle size of Bi5Nb3O15 remained unchanged. Moreover, dark spots of about 3 nm can be observed, revealing the successful modification of CQDs on Bi5Nb3O15 nanoparticles surface. The HRTEM images of 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 composite (Fig. 4f) revealed the different lattice spacing of 0.321 and 0.311 nm, corresponding to the plane (002) spacing of carbon and the plane (114) spacing of orthorhombic Bi5Nb3O15 respectively. These results indicated the presence of CQDs and Bi5Nb3O15 phases in 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites.

XPS was further employed to investigate the chemical compositions and surface characteristics of the prepared Bi5Nb3O15 and 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 samples. The full survey spectrum in Fig. 5 a showed the presence of Bi, Nb, O and C in the samples where they were expected to be. The high resolution spectra (Fig. 5b) of Bi 4f indicated the presence of two peaks of 4f7/2 at 156.7 eV and 4f5/2 at 162.1 eV which were caused by spin-orbit coupling [37]. This indicated that Bi existed mainly in the chemical state of Bi3+. Fig. 5c displayed the high resolution XPS spectrum for Nb. The binding energy peaks at 207.2 eV and 204.4 eV were attributed to Nb 3d 3/2 and Nb 3d 5/2, respectively. Compared with pure Bi5Nb3O15, Bi 4f and Nb 3d peaks of 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 shifted to higher binding energies side. This shift was probably due to interaction between CQDs and Bi5Nb3O15 [38]. In Fig. 5d, the broad peaks appeared at binding energy of 531.5 eV was attributed to the oxygen atom coordinated with Bi5Nb3O15 and oxygen bound to C atom [39]. In addition, the high resolution C 1s spectrum of 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 (Fig. 5e) showed three main peaks at 283.3, 285.3 and 287.3 eV attributed to CC, CO and CO respectively [33]. The above investigation confirmed the existence of CQDs and Bi5Nb3O15 in the CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites.

The vital importance of photoelectrochemical characteristics in photocatalysts has received great attention [40]. The transient photocurrent responses during the photocatalytic process may reflect the photo-generated charge carrier separation and transfer efficiency [41]. Photocurrents measurements were performed in the experiment by turning the light on and off after every 30 s lasting more than 6 min at an applied potential of 1.0 V. The results obtained were presented in Fig. 6 . The photocurrent values increased as the lamp was turned on, whereas, the photocurrent values decreased quickly as the lamp was turned off. The two photocatalysts exhibited similar and reasonable photocurrent behavior in the irradiation experiment. However, the 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites showed higher photocurrent. This result suggested that CQDs served as an effective charge transfer bridge that enhanced the photogenerated e− and h+ separation ability in Bi5Nb3O15, and generated higher photocurrent signal [42].

The specific surface area and pore-size distribution of the synthesized Bi5Nb3O15 and 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 samples were obtained through BET surface area analysis. As shown in Fig. 7 a, both samples showed type IV adsorption isotherms with distinct H3 hysteretic loops, in terms of IUPAC classification [43]. The corresponding BJH cumulative pore size distributions were presented in Fig, 7b. It was evident that both isotherms showed wide distribution of the pore size, which were likely originated from the irregular Bi5Nb3O15 nanosheets. The BET surface areas, average pore diameters and pore volumes of the Bi5Nb3O15, 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 and Bi5Nb3O15-1 catalysts were reported in Table 1 and Table S1, respectively. These results summarized that the specific surface area of 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 was higher than that of Bi5Nb3O15, which was ascribed to the presence of CQDs with extremely high surface area. The higher surface area of CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 could supply larger number of surface active sites for the adsorption of antibiotic pollutant, thus enhancing the rate of photocatalytic degradation process [44]. Therefore, 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites with relative high BET value is believed to be extremely beneficial for application on the photocatalytic degradation of the target pollutant.

The photocatalytic performance of as-sythesized photocatalysts was investigated for the degradation of sarafloxacin in aqueous solution under visible light irradiation. The concentrations of sarafloxacin and some of the transformation products in the reaction solution were monitored using UHPLC. Fig. 8 displayed the results of the photocatalytic degradation of sarafloxacin over Bi5Nb3O15 and CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 with different initial mass ratios of CQDs to Bi5Nb3O15. The degradation rate of sarafloxacin in the absence of photocatalyst showed relatively low degree of degradation and mineralization. When compared with pure Bi5Nb3O15, the photocatalytic degradation activity gradually increase with increasing CQDs content. Approximately 89% of sarafloxacin solution was degraded by 2.0 g L−1 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 within 180 min. Furthermore, a marginal sarafloxacin degradation rate decrease was observed when the loading of CQDs increased over 3 wt%, suggesting this content was the optimum for optimum light absorbance in the reactor system utilized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the initial sarafloxacin concentration and photocatalysts dosage
ce:section.ce:para:
It is well known that the initial concentration of substrate has great influence on the photocataytic degradation process. The role of initial concentration of sarafloxacin in the presence of 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites was investigated under Xenon illumination (Fig. 9 a). The results showed that the degradation process followed pseudo first-order degradation kinetics:
(2) ln(C 0/Ct )=kappt
where k app is the rate constant in pseudo-first order reaction, C t is the concentration of sarafloxacin at time t and C 0 is the initial concentration of sarafloxacin [45]. From the linear fitting of ln (C0/Ct ) vs reaction time, the rate constant values can be calculated. The obtained equations, k app and R2 values for the first 90 min were displayed in Table 2 . As shown in Fig. 9a and Table 2, k app value was decreased from 0.0197 to 0.0129 min−1 with increased initial sarafloxacin concentration from 0.02 to 0.06 mM suggesting deviations for first order kinetics. Decrease in k app as function of increasing sarafloxacin concentration may relate to kinetic competition for photons between intermediate degradation products and sarafloxacin compound and thus the reaction rates were slower [46]. Moreover, due to shading effect, higher sarafloxacin concentration intercepted the photons reaching ability, blocking them to reach to the surface of photocatalyst [47,48]. The number of photons absorbed by the photocatalyst was reduced and thus affecting the rate constant. Fig. 9a also revealed the degradation rate of sarafloxacin increased with initial sarafloxacin concentration. At 90 min, the rate of degradation increased from 0.394 × 10‒3 to 0.774 × 10‒3 mM min‒1 with increasing sarafloxacin concentration from 0.02 to 0.06 mM. This calculation showed that more sarafloxacin molecules were surrounded by oxidants and result in higher degradation rate.

To avoid the over use of catalysts, it is very important to determine the suitable catalyst amount, so that the as-prepared catalyst could be used with optimized degradation rate. With the aim of finding suitable catalyst amount, a group of experiments were performed at different loading of 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites under xenon illumination. As shown in Fig. 9b and Table 3 , the rate of degradation increased as the dosages increased from 0.5 to 2 g L−1, and then decreased when catalyst dosage further increased to 2.5 g L−1. Recent research found that the optimum catalyst loading is highly related to the optical parameters of catalyst in aqueous suspension [49,50]. At low 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites concentration, the quantity of catalyst was not enough to absorb sufficient visible light. To capture significant number of photons, the catalyst dosage was increased until it reaches the optimum level. However, beyond this value (2 g L−1), catalyst agglomeration and light scattering during the process might affect catalyst optical characteristics. According to the above results, the optimum 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 dose was found to be 2 g L-1 in this experiment.

UHPLC were used to detect the temporal chromatogram changes during the photocatalytic process under xenon illumination. The chromatograms were illustrated in Fig. 10 . They revealed the formation of five different intermediates. Except for sarafloxacin, compound IV and V initially increased before subsequently decreasing, indicating transformation of the intermediates. The mass spectra analysis of the five intermediates was provided in Supporting Information Fig. S1. The structures of the compounds were deduced from the mass data and molecular composition of the mother compound. The proposed degradation pathway of sarafloxacin was presented in Scheme 1 . The compound I (m/z 340) formed by hydrogen and carboxyl group cleavage. The compound II (m/z 372) and compound III (m/z 345) formed by piperazine ring opening. The compound V (m/z 318) formed by piperazine ring cleavage followed by an attack of hydroxyl radical at C-7. The compound IV (m/z 274) formed by piperazine ring and carboxyl group cleavage and further attacked by hydroxyl radical at C-3. The compound IV could also be explained by the loss of carbon dioxide from compound V. Preliminary results suggested that the formation of compound IV and V involves the reactivity of the hydroxyl radical with sarafloxacin. Furthermore, the intermediate compound V has been earlier described in literature [51], but it was the first time that compound I, II, III and IV were presented as sarafloxacin reaction intermediates during heterogeneous photocatalysis.

Based on the results of experiments, the proposed enhanced photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites in terms of degradation of sarafloxacin is displayed in Scheme 2 . Under xenon illumination, photo-generated electrons (e−) and photo-generated holes (h+) were separated from charge carriers. Under normal conditions, these photo-generated e− and h+ recombine quickly and virtually small part of these charges may take part in photodegradation experiment [52]. The introduction of CQDs in the composites could be thought as an electron pool that can trap electrons, and thus, changing the rate of e− and h+ recombination. Moreover, longer wavelength light could be absorbed by CQDs and shorter wavelength light could be emitted, owing to their upconversion photoluminescence attribute. Meanwhile, Bi5Nb3O15 nanoparticles were stimulated to generate more e− and h+ pairs. During this process, when h+ stays near the valence bands of Bi5Nb3O15, more photo-reactive sites are generated, and thus highly energetic free radicals were produced [53].

Good stability of a photocatalyst is a key parameter for practical purposes. From the economic and environmental standpoint, investigating the stability and recyclability of a photocatalyst is necessary [54,55]. The recycling stability of the photocatalyst was evaluated for five times (catalyst loading 2 g L−1 and sarafloxacin concentration 0.04 mM). As presented in Fig. 11 a, the 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites continued to maintain its photocatalytic activity after five cycles, only slight decrease was observed. The XRD spetrum of the used 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites was also recorded. As show in Fig. 11b, XRD intensity of the used 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites was lower than that of the original catalyst; no extra peaks were observed. These reusability results indicate that the nanocomposites have a suitable photocatalytic stability and great potential for their practical utility.

The practical utility of the 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites were also assessed for the long-term application. The Qujiang Chi water in Xi’an University of Technology (Xi’an, China) was used as the solvent to dissolve sarafloxacin as the reaction solution (0.04 mM). According to Fig. 12 , the degradation efficiency of sarafaloxacin for pure water and river water were 86.8% and 59.5%, respectively. The decreased degradation efficiency can be related to the existence of other substrate in real water sample that compete for the radical species. Thus, considering the obtained results, the synthesized nanocomposites demonstrate a suitable stability and practical utility. However, more specific experiments on the influence of nature organic matter, cations and anions in field water sample need to be carried out in the future to explore applicability and effectiveness of the process in field applications.

In conclusion, novel CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites were successfully synthesized using a simple hydrothermal method. The CQDs were dispersed over Bi5Nb3O15 surface and both CQDs and Bi5Nb3O15 were characterized. After adding CQDs, all CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites showed enhanced degradation efficiency under Xenon illumination. The 3 wt% CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites displayed the highest photocatalytic degradation activity. These results could be explained by the high separation efficiency of the photo-generated electron-holes pairs and the formation of more reactive radicals along with the increase of the active surface area. The photocatalytic degradation of sarafloxacin in aqueous 3 wt% suspensions of CQDs/Bi5Nb3O15 nanocomposites showed deviation from first-order kinetics and four new decomposition intermediates were identified. After five cycles of usage, the nanocomposites showed excellent stability and reusability. These results showed that this kind of nanocomposites could be applied as effective catalysts for photodegradation of fluoroquinolone antibiotics in surface water.

D. D. Dionysiou also acknowledges support from the University of Cincinnati through a UNESCO co-Chair Professor position on “Water Access and Sustainability” and the Herman Schneider Professorship in the College of Engineering and Applied Sciences. The authors want to thank James Hobson & Sanchez Thomas for providing language assistance.

Pharmaceutical Accumulation in the Environment: Prevention, Control, Health Effects, and Economic Impact
